data.title: Euro And ECB
data.description:
The euro fell nearly 1.65% in Q1, only to claw back about 1.25% in April. At one level, that looks like noise within a broader range. At another, it reflects a market that has been repeatedly and abruptly forced to reprice the policy outlook in response to shifting geopolitical and macroeconomic crosscurrents.

The ECB meets tomorrow, and a hawkish hold remains the most likely scenario. There is roughly a 10% chance of a hike priced in for this meeting. June is fully discounted for a quarter-point move. That asymmetry alone tells you something important: the bar to action tomorrow is high, but the bar to signaling is not.

The evolution of rate expectations over the past several weeks has been nothing short of dramatic. Before the escalation of the Middle East conflict, the market was still leaning toward another cut this year - about a 55% probability. Then came a sharp, violent reversal. At the peak of the repricing on April 7, three hikes were fully discounted and the market was flirting with a fourth. This seemed exaggerated, but even now, the swaps market has three hikes fully priced.

Energy prices have been the most direct transmission channel. July Brent pushed to a new contract high, $108-109 today. Even when hostilities end, the supply risk premium is unlikely to vanish quickly. This matters for Europe more than most. The region remains acutely sensitive to imported energy costs, and the second-round effects - while not yet evident in the data - are a key risk the ECB cannot dismiss.

The ECB's own survey data bear this out. Inflation expectations are rising. That explains why the market has been willing to pivot so aggressively from pricing cuts to pricing hikes. It also explains why the ECB is unlikely to push back too forcefully against current market pricing. A central bank that spent the better part of two years trying to anchor expectations is not eager to see them drift again.

The incoming data ahead of tomorrow's decision is notable. Q1 GDP is expected around 0.2%, matching Q4's pace - not recessionary, but hardly reassuring. April's preliminary CPI is expected to rise to around 3.0% from 2.6%, with a monthly gain close to 1.0% following March's 1.3%. Much of the impulse traces to energy and food; core appears broadly stable. In other words, the inflation story is visible but has not yet broadened enough to force an immediate response.

The labor market remains a source of quiet support for the hawks. The unemployment rate is expected near 6.2%, effectively matching the lowest levels of the EMU era. That gives the ECB room to maintain a tightening bias even as growth stays subdued.

From a market perspective, the euro's trajectory remains tethered to rate differentials. The US two-year premium over Germany - a relationship that tracks the exchange rate closely - narrowed sharply this year, from just above 150 basis points in mid-January to just over 100 earlier this month, the tightest in three years. It widened back toward 130, but has since compressed again to around 118. For context, it sat near 200 basis points a year ago. That compression has been one of the euro's most reliable supports. That said, the end of the Middle East war would likely see a risk-on move by investors that would buoy the euro.

The technical picture, however, has turned more cautious. The euro peaked near $1.1850 on April 17 and has since reversed lower. The initial target in the $1.1675-1.1700 area was met, but selling pressure did not look exhausted. Momentum indicators point to scope for additional losses. The next area of interest is around $1.1650.

Volatility, meanwhile, has been retreating. Three-month implied euro vol slipped to a three-month low earlier this week, just below 5.8%, against a 50-day moving average near 6.7% and a 100-day around 6.3%. The compression suggests the market is less concerned about large directional moves and more comfortable expressing views through carry and relative rates. Still, we suspect that implied vol is poised to move higher.

Finally, the consensus has quietly turned a shade less bullish. Bloomberg's latest survey puts the euro at $1.1750 at end-June and $1.19 by end-September and year-end - both a touch lower than a month ago. That shift may appear marginal, but it reflects a wider recognition that the path higher is unlikely to be linear.

The near term hinges heavily on how the ECB handles tomorrow. A hawkish hold would validate much of the recent repricing without committing to immediate action. That may be enough - for now. But with energy prices elevated, inflation expectations on the move, and geopolitical risks unresolved, the balance of risks stays fluid. The euro's recovery is real. It is not yet secure.

Original Post

data.title: Eli Lilly Stock Could Rise Sharply After Earnings
data.description:
Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) has seen its stock fall by more than 20% since peaking in mid-January and has not even participated in the latest stock market rally. That may be about to change, at least for the short term. Based on options positioning after the company reports results, the stock could head back toward $1,000.

The options market has become so bearish on Eli Lilly shares that it may actually prove bullish. First-quarter results are expected to be strong, with earnings rising by 99.5% to $6.66 per share, on revenue growth of 38.4% to $17.6 billion. Meanwhile, analysts estimate second-quarter earnings growth to slow to 28.1%, to $8.08 per share, with revenue growth down to 26.4%, to $19.66 billion.

Elevated Implied Volatility

Still, put positioning appears to heavily outweigh call positioning, resulting in a negative overall delta and gamma. Normally, options positioning is not an overall problem except when heading into earnings. Currently, implied volatility levels are very high, running above 80%, and are due to fall sharply after the company reports results.

For example, an $865 strike price put expiring on May 1 currently has a premium of about $28 and an IV around 100%. As a result, the stock would need to fall to $837 for the owner of those puts to start earning a profit. But after earnings, the implied volatility for both puts and calls will fall sharply and could even be cut in half. That means the put premium could also decline to roughly $16. So the options immediately start to lose value.

Options Positioning May Favor A Rally

As a result, put premiums may very well start to decay across all strike prices, and market makers may find themselves overhedged against put positioning, potentially forcing them to buy back shares. Holders of puts at lower strike prices may begin unwinding their positions, potentially adding more upward pressure to the stock.

Additionally, a put gamma between the $830 and $850 strike prices may offer support. As the stock declines to support dealer hedging flows, it may require the buying of shares, helping to stabilize the price. However, if it rallies after the result, it could be heading much higher, with the chart showing the largest number of calls across expiration at $1,000.

Also, the at-the-money put option, mentioned earlier, and put options with lower strike prices will have a really tough time seeing the premium rise enough to make them profitable. Unless the company releases horrible results that are truly shocking to the market, pushing the stock prices through all that option-related put support. Therefore, the options positioning appears to favor a move higher, even if it only proves to be short-lived.

Technical Support And Resistance

The technical chart doesn't lay out the support and resistance levels presented in the options position, as one might like. But that is also because the stock had such a violent move higher in October and into November. However, one can see some support from prior highs around $850 and another layer of support from a November gap around $825. Additionally, there are signs of a potential bullish divergence forming with the RSI.

While Lilly has already declined meaningfully over the last several months, the heavy bearish positioning sets up what appears to favor a rebound, potentially toward the $970–$1,000 range—even if it proves to be a post-earnings bounce in the longer term.

data.title: Progressive Stock Crash Explained: Strong Business, Falling Shares?
data.description:
Follow Seeking Profits on Seeking Alpha!

Read Seeking Profits' Article on Seeking Alpha!

Sign Up For Premium Today!

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is offered for information purposes only. Unless stated otherwise, any and all individuals participating in the video are third parties that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Unless stated otherwise, the views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole.

The accuracy and completeness of content shared cannot be guaranteed. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment advisor, or investment bank.

Nicole Benjamin: Hey, everybody. It's Nicole Benjamin, your host here at Seeking Alpha to bring to you another episode of our series, Portfolio Pulse, where, as the name suggests, we're going to be keeping a pulse to all the big financial moves happening in the market. Now, joining us for today's episode is Scott of Seeking Profits. So, thank you so much, Scott, for joining us today.

Scott: Thanks for having me, Nicole. Excited to be here.

NB: Absolutely. Now, Scott, I see you've been on Seeking Alpha since 2013. So, tell us a little bit more about that. How'd you get on the platform? How'd you get into investing? What's your investment style? All those things.

SP: Yeah. No. As you said, I've been on Seeking Alpha for like 13 years now. I've worked in the investment management industry for most of that time. And I did particularly enjoy writing and discussing my ideas and providing ideas for readers and getting interactions to their comments and starting a conversation. So, when I think about how I invest and how I – the ideas I tend to share, I'm very fundamental in my thinking versus maybe a more technical or quantitative.

I tend to focus on value stocks versus growth generally. So, I like to get into the weeds of the financials, see how a company is doing, think about what the macroeconomic environment may do to that company, project out what they'll earn and see where I see good values that maybe the market is under-appreciating and see those as good buy opportunities and so forth.

NB: Now, I see in your most recent article that you're talking about all things Progressive, PGR. So, I'm looking here and Progressive seems to be doing, well, they're actually doing fairly well financially. So, my first question for you is, I guess, what is the gap between how the business is doing and how the stock is trading when it has recently dropped about 30%?

SP: Yeah. So, Progressive is exactly the kind of company I like to look at because when you look at its underlying financials, it's about as profitable as it's ever been. But as you said, the stock has come down 30% or so from its highs. And a lot of that has to do with investors' expectations for the future. And in particular, if you think about what's been going on in the auto insurance space, post-COVID auto insurers struggled to make money because they had to pay a lot more in claims when car prices got more expensive.

They then began raising their premiums, as any of us who have a car knows that premiums had gone up quite a bit, which has restored their profitability, but as that has happened, there's now concern that we're hitting the peak in profits. So, whenever you see profit growth slow or potentially turn, that sometimes can create a lot of negative sentiment around a stock, which is what's happened here. And my view is that actually while margins may be about as good as they can get, people are too scared of the future and that the future won't be as bad as it will be and the stock is now discounting to negative of an environment. And that's what's created a good buy opportunity. It's that negative sentiment that has gotten ahead of what we're seeing in the actual financials.

NB: Now, you also mentioned in your article this combined ratio of 86.4%. So, what does this number tell us about how much profit they make on every dollar in the insurance space as they're selling?

SP: Yeah. So, when you ever are looking at a financial, an insurance company, the combined ratio is about the first thing you want to look at. So, if you think about it, when you have, let's say you get a car premium policy and you pay a $100 in premiums. Combined ratio is how much of that is the insurer actually paying back. So, when you get into an accident and they have to pay a claim, how much of that is being paid?

So, if you have a combined ratio of 86.4%, that means that if they get a $100 in premiums, on average they're paying out to the customers and all their costs, $86.40. And they have about a 13% or $13 profit margin that is the amount they're actually making on the premiums.

NB: We've all seen car prices go up, go down, all these things, especially post-COVID, as you said. So, how does inflation in the real world, in real time, start to show up in Progressive's profit margins?

SP: When Progressive writes its premium, it's policy for you and says, I'll sell you your car insurance for $600, say. They have an assumption there of when there are accidents, they have an assumption of how many accidents there will be and then what will be the cost of those accidents, the cost when a car is totaled of replacing the car and so forth. That's going to be based on the estimate of what car prices are right now. If car prices go up 15% in a short period of time, suddenly the cost of replacing parts and fixing up the cars will be worse than they had modeled. And so, they're actually, even if there's the same number of accidents, the severity of those accidents may be worse.

And they end up paying out more and they have the same amount of premiums that compresses their combined ratio. And that's what we saw during COVID what happened is used car prices rose like 40%. And so, in a very short period of time, they're paying out a lot more claims than they had anticipated. They’ve since raised premiums. And the concern has been, one of the reasons why the stock has struggled is with tariffs and other pricing pressures. The fear that car prices could go up and you have a repeat of that. Interestingly, like new car prices haven't moved, they moved less than 1% over the past year. Used car prices are only at 3%. And that type of inflation, they can match. It's when you start seeing 8%, 10%, 15% price increases that they start to have problems.

NB: Now, I see here that Progressive has an AA- rating, which, I mean, you noted that Progressive is very liquid. So, I mean, that's a great thing, but why is it so important for a car insurer to keep their savings account safe and easy to access in this case?

SP: Yeah. So, when you think about any of the insurers, and in some ways it's like how Berkshire Hathaway and Buffett's made all their money is, that they take all these premiums and then they can invest those premiums and then they earn money on that until they pay you back with claims and so forth. Generally speaking, the longer they'll be holding the money, this would be like a life insurer, where you know you won't be paying a claim for 30 years on average, you can take a bit more risk in your investment portfolio because you won't be paying out claims tomorrow.

Auto insurance is much shorter in nature, right? Typically policies are 6 to 12 months. They're facing accidents quite often. And then when you have an event like the hurricane or a significant storm, you could have several hundred million dollars that you have to pay out right away. When you have to pay claims out more quickly, you need to make sure that the money you'd save is liquid and you can easily sell-out your investments like you own treasuries or high quality corporate bonds that you could easily sell to then pay out the claims.

So, generally speaking, in that insurance sector, the quicker you pay claims, the more safety you need in your investment portfolio. And that's why Progressive has about the safest portfolio of any insurance company I cover.

NB: Progressive is also known for special dividends. So, what should investors be thinking about when they're receiving these occasional payouts versus something that's more regular, like on a monthly basis?

SP: When Progressive – when your company is determining what they want to return to investors, they set that regular dividend at the level they can know they can sustain no matter what. Where special dividends or buybacks is much more easier to go up or down. So, as an investor, you should never rely on what the previous year's special dividends have been is it can change. And so, particularly for a company like Progressive, when you're an insurance company, depending on the year, your earnings can move quite a bit with, particularly with hurricanes.

So, those catastrophic events where, last year, we had almost, we had no hurricanes hit the U.S. So, they had no hurricane losses, which means they had a greater profit than they probably would in a normal year, which left them with more money at the end of the year. So, they gave a bigger special dividend. When I like to look at any of the insurance companies, what I try to do is assume what do they make in an average catastrophe year, knowing some years are better, some years are worse. And then that probably is a sense of what that average special dividend or buyback can be, but there'll be years where it's less, years where it's more.

So, I think whenever you're investing in a company, you can focus on relying on that ordinary dividend, but then view that special dividend to say it will toggle up or down and don't bank on it being the same every year.

NB: Okay. Well, I have one more question before we get out of here for you. Just off the top of your head, you had a Buy rating for Progressive, and you also talked about this negative sentiment with other investors and what's going on in the market. So, for the future, what would you like to see from Progressive that would potentially tip the scales for you and make that a Strong Buy rating?

SP: What I really look for is, if you see some negative news and the stock doesn't go down or actually goes up, that's a way of registering that sentiment has reached an extreme, right? When you stop going down on bad news, it means all the bad news has been priced into the stock and even a little bit of good news, it can start to recover sharply. So, I need that confidence that, I'm trying to make my Buy recommendations out 12, 18 months. And I do think over that period of time, we'll see sentiment turn, but I don't know if that sentiment is going to turn today or turn in three months.

So, I need to see a little bit more evidence that we've got as negative as we can get on that sentiment front and that'll probably move me to a Strong Buy.

NB: Alright. Well, perfect. Thank you so much, Scott, for joining us today. And for everybody that's listening in, go ahead, click the follow button on Seeking Profits page, and also take a look at the articles, see if Progressive might be right for your portfolio. And we'll see you here next time. Thank you so much.

Follow Seeking Profits on Seeking Alpha!

Read Seeking Profits' Article on Seeking Alpha!

Sign Up For Premium Today!

data.title: April FOMC U.S. Index Levels For Dow Jones, Nasdaq And S&P 500
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Could the fun be over for US Stock Markets?

It is a perpetual question for investors, who are constantly interacting with thousands of catalysts and data points. The long term says that markets are always more resilient, but traders are looking to capitalize in the short term.

While the direction remains very uncertain, volatility will persist over the next 24 hours.

The first major catalyst is expected about 2.5 hours from now, with the FOMC Rate Decision (100% priced unchanged). As always, communications at the press conference are what will move markets the most, so traders should keep their bullets for the 14:30 (ET) event.

This will be Powell's final press conference as Federal Reserve Chairman, so expect some additional thoughts and words which could have a decent market impact – The Fed Chair could still have some tough words on inflation expectations.

Other volatility-shaking catalysts for stocks include the highly anticipated and feared Mag 7 earnings, with Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT) reporting after the close.

Projections are for sustained record earnings, but investors will be particularly attentive to whether extremely elevated infrastructure spending is beginning to yield a return on investment.

The pressure point for investors and market sentiment overall is to see if AI really turns out to be as profitable as the trillions invested require it to be.

We will get the answer for this throughout the years, but elevated volatility and expectations could still have an effect on present risk appetite. This is what happened in October 2025 and led to the gigantic AI/Tech crash.

Finally, the US-Iran impasse continues, and it seems that Iran is slowly feeling the pressure from the Strait of Hormuz blockade. While the strategy hurts the global economy, it is a decent counterattack on what the Islamic regime was imposing on Gulf oil exporters.

This is why President Trump wasn't opposed to maintaining the ceasefire, and the strategy should pressure negotiations promptly – the issue, however, is that oil markets are not so patient, and WTI crude is now well back above the triple-digit mark, trading around $105!

Let's dive into intraday charts and trading levels for the Dow Jones Industrial Average, Nasdaq Composite, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

The stock market is trading in a low-volatility, profit-taking environment, which is dragging most sectors lower.

Only the eternally strong semiconductors are mean-reverting higher after the past session's tumble, bringing Nasdaq back higher – Expect this picture to change tomorrow depending on the earnings.

Dow Jones 2H Chart and FOMC Trading Levels

The Dow Jones officially broke its 500-point consolidation to the downside, which dampens the short-term momentum.

Still, the 2H 200-period MA is acting as support, and traders will have to monitor if it serves as support or breaks after the FOMC – look at 48,860 in that event.

  • Below points to ~48,400 – stronger selling could easily turn to 48,000.
  • Rebounding from there could regain 49,500 and even test 50,000 (on a dovish outlook from Powell)

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Major Pivot – 49,000 to 49,100
  • Weekend Gap Fill Resistance 49,500 - 49,600
  • 49,900 to 50,000 Resistance and Early 2026 Highs
  • All-Time Highs 50,544

Support Levels

  • 2H 200-period MA 48,860
  • Momentum Support 48,500
  • Pivotal Support at 48,000 (Mid-term Bearish below)
  • Mini Support 47,400 to 47,600

Nasdaq 2H Chart and Trading Levels

While remaining the only index higher in this morning's action, Nasdaq is stuck in a 150-point range right around 27,000.

Look for breakouts above and below that range, which should lead to strong follow-through.

If sellers take the advantage, look at the 26,100 - 26,200 previous all-time highs for a retest.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Momentum Pivot at 27,000 - 27,150 (testing)
  • Record highs 27,420
  • 27,500 micro-resistance
  • 28,000 Major psychological resistance (and channel highs)

Support Levels

  • Mini-support 26,600 to 26,750
  • Prior ATH Support 26,200 to 26,300
  • War Support 25,000 to 25,250
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 2H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 officially broke its upward trend but remains comfortably above 7,100 in its consolidation.

  • Breaking the psychological level should, like Nasdaq, extend back to the previous all-time highs (~7,020).
  • In the event of a bounce, look at 7,230 as a target, and to confirm, look for a clean break of 7,180.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Mini-resistance 7,150 - 7,160 (rejecting)
  • New all-time resistance 7,180 - 7,200
  • Next key potential resistance 7,200
  • Mini-channel highs 7,250

Support Levels

  • Weekend gap 7,100 Pivot
  • Prior ATH Pivotal support 7,020 to 7,050
  • Minor Support 6,880 to 6,900
  • Pivotal Support 6,750 to 6,770
  • 6,300 psychological level (war lows)

Keep track of WTI Crude and the latest headlines throughout the week to stay ahead of the curve, with investors still confused about US-Iran negotiations.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

data.title: Outlook For Oil As Strait Of Hormuz Closure, UAE Exit From OPEC Roils Energy Markets
data.description:
With the Strait of Hormuz still closed and the UAE signaling plans to leave OPEC, the oil outlook is becoming more uncertain. Bart Melek, Head of Commodity Strategy with TD Securities, explains what this could mean for energy prices and how long elevated costs could last.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: The Iran War and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has jolted the global energy markets. And now there's further uncertainty as the UAE has announced its plans to exit OPEC. Joining us now to discuss what this all means for the price of oil is Bart Melek, Head of Commodity Strategy with TD Securities.

Bart, great to have you on the show again.

Bart Melek: Well, thank you very much for having me.

Anthony Okolie: As you know, lot's happening in the news. We'll start with the news of UAE planning to exit OPEC next month. What sort of impact could that have?

Bart Melek: I think in the short run, not very much. Typically, the issue with OPEC has been to keep supply under wraps-- in essence, make sure there isn't rampant overproduction and discipline along a quota system. But given the Gulf War right now, we're seeing some 8 million barrel per day deficit. We're quite sure there are a few million barrels of supply that has been destroyed due to the conflict.

All these producers in OPEC, Saudi Arabia-- and even when United Arab Emirates is out-- they will have to produce at full speed. And that's probably not going to change for at least a year and a half to 2 years. So the immediate impact is not significant.

Everyone is going to try to produce as much as they possibly can. And we're not going to worry about adhering to any quota or being retaliated against by Saudi Arabia if you overproduce. And they happen to have some million barrels of more capacity than their quota and they want to deploy that, clearly.

Anthony Okolie: OK. Now of course, the Strait of Hormuz remains closed. What sort of scenarios do you see potentially playing out here for the energy sector?

Bart Melek: Well, it's getting a little late in the day for things to be left benign. And what do I mean by this? Even if the war stops today and tanker traffic moves through that chokepoint, it's going to take a few months for tankers to go from the Straits of Hormuz, the Middle East there, to some Asian destinations.

And we are noticing a pretty severe decline in the amount of oil at sea available to be distributed to consumers. And I suspect that in the next two, three weeks, we are going to start hitting some hard physical constraints and we could still see significantly higher prices. If this thing continues longer than that, it could be even more severe. So we could, theoretically, without any difficulty, see $150 Brent.

Anthony Okolie: OK. Now, it's not just oil that's been impacted by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. What other parts of the commodity market are also being impacted?

Bart Melek: Well, I recently wrote a piece on fertilizers and food inflation. And fertilizers, they are not necessarily talked about a lot, but they are an incredibly important factor of production for agriculture. And the Middle East produces a lot of ammonia, urea, LNG-- which fertilizer producers take the nitrogen out to include in fertilizers-- sulfur, and other additives that are used as nutrients for farmers. And that also ultimately means that with prices sky high and de facto shortages, that we will see less intensive use of fertilizers, meaning that crop yields next season, upon harvest, may be less than they were in previous years. And that could very well mean higher food prices.

Diesel is extremely important for farming-- and not only transporting but also drying and processing. We are seeing stories anecdotally at this point-- statistics are hard to get-- that some farmers across the world-- in Australia, to mention one destination-- where they're not planting for the lack of diesel. We're seeing fishing fleets around the world not deploying their fleets to gather seafood and fish, which means you will need to rely on protein from other sources, which tend to feed on grain. And so you could have that cascading effect--

Anthony Okolie: Seeing that wider effect--

Bart Melek: --on food prices, I think.

Anthony Okolie: Now, it's not just food, but gold has been on a bit of a roller coaster recently. They've pulled back from highs. What's happening in the precious metals sector-- for the precious metal, actually?

Bart Melek: Well, precious metals have suffered because of this war. And it's contrary to what most people would automatically assume. And the reason is, you associate gold with inflation. And the argument goes, if inflation is high, gold will tend to do well, and that is very, very true.

Now the trick is, in order for that to happen, you're going to have to have monetary authorities that sort of ignore inflation. So typically, if inflation is running at 2.5%, a little higher, central banks aren't particularly worried and they might want to keep growth strong. And the argument is-- well, you know, what's 1%? No big deal. And interest rates could very well be accommodative and negative in real terms. And that concern has propagated this whole debasement trade.

But now the fear is that it's not going to be 2.5%. It could very well be 4%-plus. If we have an additional oil shock and energy price shock, it could be higher still. And in those circumstances, central banks probably have learned a lesson from late '70s, early '80s, and might want to be restrictive, meaning real interest rates quite high. And meaning that gold will likely have a difficult time because carry costs and opportunity costs are high relative to the fixed income market.

And it always is the case that really, in the end, gold is a function of interest rates. When central banks are irresponsible and allow inflation to fester, gold does well. But when they tighten up, as we know after Mr. Volcker took over, we had high inflation and gold collapsed for a while. Then, at least for now, we think we could see a correction.

Same with silver, but silver has an additional problem. It's an industrial metal, as well-- 60% of it. And if we get any sort of form of stagflation, a weak economy, meaning that silver demand for industrial uses-- everything from catalysts to chemical, industry. Even jewelry-- people who don't have jobs don't tend to buy a lot of jewelry, and that could weigh on silver as well.

Longer term, we're very, very optimistic on both. But I think the trick is going to be to keep the faith over the next few months.

Original Post

data.title: KalVista: 'Strong Buy' As Acquired By Chiesi And Positive KONFIDENT-KID Outcome
data.description:
The last time I spoke about KalVista Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (KALV), it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "KalVista: Next Stage Of Growth For EKTERLY Lies With Pediatric Label Expansion." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was doing particularly well in terms of its sales with the first and only oral on-demand hereditary angioedema [HAE] therapy, EKTERLY. I mentioned that it had given unaudited financials showing that this drug was able to generate revenue of $49 million for the full year 2025. I had given this stock a Strong Buy rating, and this is where I'm going to keep it. There is one main reason to do so, which is that it has been bought out by Chiesi Group for $27 per share in cash.

This is huge because it represents a market value of $1.9 billion. The truth is that this buyout makes sense, because the company did make some additional progress since I last wrote about it. In particular, what I also mentioned in the prior article was that the company was in the process of evaluating the use of EKTERLY as an on-demand HAE treatment for pediatric patients ages 2 to 11 in the ongoing phase 3 KONFIDENT-KID trial. Well, the good news on this front is that the company was able to report positive interim results from this phase 3 study and that such data was presented at the 2026 Global Angioedema Leadership Conference.

The sales definitely happened because on March 25, 2026, it reported that for the eight months ending December 31, 2025, it generated $49.1 million in sales of EKTERLY. Lastly, there are expansions already underway. The launch of this drug in Germany was following a similar pattern to that of the United States, for starters. In addition, the company had launched the drug in Japan with its partner Kaken Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., and the beauty of this is that EKTERLY is the first and only oral on-demand therapy in that territory for HAE patients. With several new key developments moving the launch of EKTERLY in the right direction, plus the recent buyout of this company by Chiesi, I believe that I should conclude this stock with a Strong Buy rating that I had set on it last time around.

Oral Disintegrating Tablet Formulation In KONFIDENT-KID and Positive Interim Moves EKTERLY Towards Intended Expansion

As I mentioned above, KalVista Pharmaceuticals has been able to make additional progress in other key areas since I last wrote about it. One of these updates includes that of reporting positive interim data from its phase 3 KONFIDENT-KID trial using its oral on-demand HAE therapy EKTERLY [sebetralstat] for the treatment of Hereditary Angioedema [HAE] patients. Before going over the interim analysis of this late-stage study, plus any catalysts to come about from this program, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Hereditary Angioedema [HAE] is characterized as a rare genetic disorder where there are significant bouts of swelling that a person experiences all over their body. For the most part, the swelling occurs on major parts of the body like:

  • Hands
  • Feet
  • Face.

On the other hand, it is not just a disorder that occurs solely in those areas I just noted above. Swelling [edema] can happen in the internal part of a person's body, like their digestive tract and/or airways. The thing is that the swelling attacks occur as a result of a chain reaction. First and foremost, there is a dysfunction of the C1-inhibitor [C1-INH] protein. That's because this protein is supposed to inhibit serine proteases like kallikrein and activated factor XII [FxIIa]. With the C1-INH unable to break down kallikrein, this causes a chain reaction to excessive bradykinin production, and excessive amounts of this are bad because it binds to B2 receptors on vascular endothelial cells. Why is this bad? That's because it then leads to excessive vascular permeability and then subsequent swelling [edema] attacks. There are three types of HAE, and they are:

  • Type I HAE - most common type of HAE, accounting for up to 85% of cases, is a result of the body not being able to produce enough C1-INH.
  • Type II HAE - the next common type of HAE, the body does produce significant amounts of C1-INH; however, it doesn't function the way it is supposed to.
  • Type III HAE - Normal C1-INH levels exist, but despite this, swelling occurs for other reasons that happen in the body.

The global hereditary angioedema therapeutics market size is projected to reach $8.18 billion by 2033. This is a very large market opportunity, and the truth is that despite prophylaxis therapies being available, on-demand therapies have continued to show growth [KalVista Pharmaceuticals Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #14]. The company appears to be doing quite well based on its 2025 full-year performance, having achieved $49.1 million in sales of EKTERLY.

The thing is that EKTERLY has been approved for several territories as an on-demand HAE therapy for patients ages 12 and older. The goal of the company was to expand the pool of patients that it could go after with this drug. Having said that, this is where the phase 3 open-label KONFIDENT-KID study comes into play. This trial recruited a total of 36 children ages 2 to 11 and then gave them EKTERLY as a therapy. There are two aspects to consider here regarding why this program is highly bullish, and they are:

  • Taking the program further for these children by using a proprietary oral disintegrating tablet [ODT].
  • Recruitment for this phase 3 trial was only set to be 24 patients; however, the demand was so huge that it is ultimately set to enroll 36 patients.

With a cut-off date of December 15, 2025, it was noted that EKTERLY [sebetralstat] was able to indeed act as a significant on-demand HAE therapy for them. First of all, it accomplished the ability to have treatment given to them within an average of 25 mins. It was said that about 67% of the 175 HAE attacks were treated within the first hour. The average time that these patients experienced relief with respect to their swelling was within a 1.5-hour period. However, for the swelling to actually be gone entirely [complete resolution], this didn't happen until over a 12-hour period. Why is this data crucial? Especially for these pediatric patients ages 2 to 11? The reason has to do with the currently available therapies. Current therapies involve intravenous [IV] infusion and subcutaneous [SC] administration. Not only is it more complicated, but the average time for a patient to actually be treated goes up. This is because it takes longer to eventually get these types of therapies administered to these pediatric patients. A convenient disintegrating oral on-demand HAE therapy like EKTERLY could solve this challenge.

The thing is that KalVista made significant progress with this program. As a matter of fact, it was able to complete enrollment one year ahead of its expected schedule. Not only that, but there was a major catalyst that was approaching for this company, which is that it was set to file a supplemental New Drug Application [sNDA] of EKTERLY for the treatment of these patients with HAE in Q3 of 2026. Should this review eventually go well, then it anticipates being able to receive U.S. marketing approval of this plasma kallikrein inhibitor in 2027. This is very bullish, because it is possible to achieve a first here. If approved by the FDA for these pediatric patients ages 2 to 11, then it would be the first and only oral on-demand therapy for them.

Data With KONFIDENT-KID And A Significant Sales Milestone Likely Led To Acquisition

The thing is that KalVista Pharmaceuticals was firing on multiple fronts. I went over the achievement it made with the positive interim results it obtained in the phase 3 open-label KONFIDENT-KID study using EKTERLY as an on-demand treatment for pediatric HAE patients ages 2 to 11. A second aspect is the revenue that the company has achieved since the launch of the drug. Consider that the drug didn't launch officially until the 2nd half of 2025. From the commercial launch of EKTERLY on July 7, 2025, through December 31, 2025, net product revenue achieved was $49.1 million. Plus, it was able to launch and will launch the drug in several other territories. In my opinion, I believe that all of these aspects collectively led to the buyout I'm about to go over below.

It was just announced that an international biopharmaceutical group by the name of Chiesi entered into a strategic agreement to acquire KalVista Pharmaceuticals for $27 per share in a cash transaction. This deal values this biotech at $1.9 billion. This move was specifically spearheaded by Chiesi's business unit, known as "Chiesi Global Rare Diseases." The thing is that Chiesi wanted to bolster its rare disease product portfolio and notes to have the reach to give further patient access to EKTERLY for starters. Secondly, the acquisition of this candidate is expected to allow it to meet its goal of being able to achieve €6 billion in revenues as part of its 2030 strategic goal initiative. Customary to all conditions being met as part of this acquisition agreement, the deal is expected to close in Q3 2026.

Financials

According to the 10-KT SEC filing, KalVista Pharmaceuticals had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $300.2 million as of December 31, 2025. The key thing to note is that this was a 10-KT SEC filing because the company changed its fiscal quarter to end on December 31, 2025. Thus, there is an 8-month transition period from May 1, 2025, to December 31, 2025, and it reported its financials within condensed consolidated financial statements. The truth is that the company has stayed afloat thanks to raising cash through several financial tools over the years through the issuance of stock, pre-funded warrants, convertible debt, and royalty financing agreements.

With that said, it was likely going to have to find a way to raise additional funds in the next several months. What makes me say that? Well, that's because in its 10-KT SEC filing, it gave guidance that it believes its cash on hand is only enough to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. The thing is that the company had a sales agreement in place with TD Securities LLC, to which it could offer and sell from time to time as needed up to an aggregate of $100 million worth of shares. As of December 31, 2025, it offered and sold no shares under this ATM agreement. The thing is that with the expected acquisition of KalVista by Chiesi [expected to close in Q3 of 2026], it likely doesn't have to worry about cash on hand anymore so long as the deal goes through.

Risks to Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in KalVista Pharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing program of the phase 3 open-label KONFIDENT-KID study using orally disintegrating on-demand HAE therapy EKTERLY for the treatment of pediatric patients ages 2 to 11. The goal of the company is to be able to file an sNDA of this therapy for the treatment of these HAE patients in Q3 of 2026.

The risk here is that even though the interim analysis proved that the oral disintegrating tablet of EKTERLY was shown to work well, there is no assurance that the FDA is even going to accept the sNDA submission in its present form. Nor that ultimately the FDA is going to approve the expanded label of EKTERLY as an oral on-demand HAE therapy for these pediatric patients ages 2 to 11.

The second risk to consider is in terms of the revenues generated thus far for EKTERLY. For the 8 months ending December 31, 2025, KalVista reported global net product revenues of this therapy to be $49.1 million. The first risk here is that even though this drug started off strong, there is no assurance that the sales it has generated thus far with it are going to continue in the coming quarters. Especially when you consider that refills accounted for more than 50% of the revenues that were generated in Q4 2025. Secondly, there is an expectation that the company could potentially achieve >$1.8 billion in net product revenues of EKTERLY [KalVista Pharmaceuticals Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #17]. Such a move is intended to happen if patients convert from injectable generics over to an oral on-demand treatment like EKTERLY.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that such a trend switch will happen, if at all. In turn, this means the projected revenue expected from this move may not even occur.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the strategic transaction of Chiesi acquiring KalVista Pharmaceuticals for $27 per share in cash [a deal valued at $1.9 billion]. The thing is that there is already one hurdle out of the way, which is that this move was already approved by the Board of Directors of both companies. The problem is that this transaction still has to pass customary closing conditions in order to be completed in Q3 of 2026. Along with the fact that it must pass regulatory scrutiny as well.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that this transaction will actually close as intended. Should the deal collapse for any reason, then KalVista will have to go back to operating as a separate entity and rely on itself to continue to commercialize EKTERLY as an oral on-demand HAE therapy.

Conclusion

KalVista Pharmaceuticals was able to achieve a significant milestone since I wrote about it in my prior Seeking Alpha article. It had released positive interim results from the phase 3 KONFIDENT-KID study using EKTERLY as an on-demand oral therapy to treat HAE for pediatric patients ages 2 to 11. This was evidenced by the fact that the average time to treatment for these patients was 25 minutes. Plus, a majority of the attacks were treated within a 1-hour period for them. This is going to allow for the filing of the sNDA of EKTERLY so that it can be expanded to be included for these pediatric patients ages 2 to 11. At the present time, it is only approved as an oral on-demand HAE therapy for patients ages 12 and older across several territories.

Speaking of which, it has been approved for these particular patients in several territories beyond the United States, like the European Union, the United Kingdom, Switzerland, Australia, Singapore, and Japan. To take it one step further, it is expected to submit several other applications to global health authorities across the globe to expand it in the coming years. With substantial revenues generated from EKTERLY for the 8 months ending December 31, 2025, of $49.1 million, plus the acquisition of it by Chiesi for a market value of $1.9 billion, I believe that I should close out this company's stock with a Strong Buy rating. Should this acquisition be closed in Q3 of 2026, then EKTERLY belongs to Chiesi for further advancement.

data.title: Manufactured Housing: The Wide Moat Hidden In Plain Sight
data.description:
Warren Buffett built his legacy investment practices on a simple premise: investing in businesses with durable demand, strong economics, and long-term relevance.

In other words, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) was built on businesses that enjoyed wide economic moats.

While most investors associate Mr. Buffett with iconic holdings like Wells Fargo and Coca-Cola, one of his most strategic and underappreciated investments is centered on America’s housing crisis.

In 2003, when Berkshire Hathaway acquired Clayton Homes for $1.7 billion, it was a little-known deal; however, these days, considering the ongoing affordability crisis, Buffett’s bet seems like a stroke of genius.

Today, affordability has become one of the most pressing economic challenges in the U.S., as traditional homebuilders have struggled to deliver supply at price points accessible to the median American, leaving a growing gap between income and homeownership.

Of course, manufactured housing fills that gap because these homes offer significantly lower cost per square foot, faster construction timelines, comparable quality with modern building standards, and a pathway to ownership for millions priced out of traditional housing.

The Demand Drivers: A Perfect Storm of Tailwinds

I know of no other property sector that has such strong demand drivers, and one of the most obvious is the sticky customer base.

Residents typically own their homes and lease the land, creating high friction to move. The result is annual move-outs of just ~0.4%, stable occupancy across economic cycles, and predictable, growing net operating income (NOI).

Another demand driver, as I mentioned earlier, is affordability: manufactured housing costs 50% less to build than traditional single-family rentals. In addition, manufactured housing space is 25% larger than multifamily housing.

In a world where housing costs continue to rise, manufactured housing delivers more for less, and that equation is hard to beat.

Third, as already mentioned, the U.S. is currently short nearly 4 million single-family homes, a deficit that continues to widen.

Traditional construction faces labor shortages, rising material costs, and lengthy development timelines. Manufactured housing, by contrast, offers speed, efficiency, and scalability, positioning it as a critical solution to the ongoing supply gap.

Fourth, the aging of America (or so-called silver tsunami) is a powerful tailwind in which the 55+ population is expected to grow by ~14% from 2025 to 2040. Roughly 10,000 Americans turn 65 every day, and these consumers are downsizing, seeking affordability, and prioritizing lifestyle and community.

Fifth, population growth continues to concentrate in high-demand Sunbelt states such as Florida, Texas, Arizona, North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia, Tennessee, and Nevada. These markets offer lower taxes, strong job growth, favorable climates, and relative affordability.

Manufactured housing communities are often located directly in the path of this “sunbelt” migration, capturing long-term population inflows.

The Supply Constraint: Where the Moat Is Built

Demand tells one side of the story, and supply tells the other, and this is where I find the most obvious moat for the manufactured housing sector.

Despite strong and growing demand (I just listed these 5 demand drivers above), new manufactured housing development has been extremely limited for over two decades. Why is that?

1. NIMBYism (“Not In My Backyard”): Local resistance remains a major obstacle to new community development.

2. Restrictive Zoning & Regulation: Many municipalities impose zoning barriers that limit or prohibit manufactured housing altogether.

3. Local Control Over Development: Even when federal policy supports affordable housing, local governments control zoning, creating structural bottlenecks.

4 Business Models Built on Scarcity

The manufactured housing sector is dominated by a group of public REITs that have built scale, operating expertise, and durable cash flow models over decades. These three players are Sun Communities (SUI), Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS), UMH Properties (UMH), and Flagship Communities (MHC.UN)

Each follows a similar core model but with important strategic differences. Let’s dive in…

Founded in 1975, Sun Communities began as a small owner-operator of manufactured housing communities in Michigan. The company went public in 1993 and evolved from a pure-play manufactured housing operator into a diversified platform that includes:

  • Manufactured housing communities (294 communities)
  • RV resorts (166 communities)
  • UK-manufactured housing (53 parks).

Today, SUI has interests in 513 communities (179,000 sites) across North America (35 states and a Canadian Province), making it one of the largest players in the space.

Sun’s model is built on scale and diversification, and at its core, the company is a land-lease business that monetizes high-occupancy communities with steady rent growth.

What differentiates Sun is its ability to layer in multiple verticals, creating a broader lifestyle platform while still anchored in affordable housing.

The company recently sold its Safe Harbor marina business (for $5.65 bn at 21x FFO) to focus on deleveraging the balance sheet (repaid $3.3 bn in debt) and upgraded to BBB+ (S&P) and Baa2 (Moody’s).

This resilient demand and high barriers to entry have resulted in consistent and cycle-tested organic NOI growth. For over 25 years, every individual year or rolling 4-quarter period recorded positive same-property NOI growth. Since 2000, Sun’s average annual same-property NOI growth was 5.2% (~230 bps greater than multifamily REITs).

As shown below, SUI has maintained steady earnings and dividend growth, suggesting the company has a strong moat (more on that later).

Equity LifeStyle Properties traces its roots back to the early 1990s and is widely viewed as the best-in-class operator in the manufactured housing sector. The company has grown through strategic acquisitions, premium community development, and a focus on high-quality, amenity-rich properties.

ELS owns 453 properties (173,371 sites) in 35 states and 1 Canadian Province. It owns and operates the highest-quality portfolio of manufactured home communities, recreational vehicle resorts, campgrounds, and marinas, with the majority located in Sunbelt retirement and vacation destinations.

ELS has built a reputation for disciplined capital allocation and operational excellence, consistently delivering high occupancy and steady NOI growth. The company has been a top performer in dividend growth, with a 10-year CAGR of 10.6%.

Founded in 1968, UMH Properties is one of the oldest players in the manufactured sector, with a long history rooted in workforce housing. Unlike its larger peers, UMH has historically focused on secondary and tertiary markets, affordable housing for working-class residents, and incremental, organic growth.

UMH’s portfolio consists of 145 manufactured home communities containing approximately 27,100 developed homesites located across AL, FL, GA, IN, MD, MI, NJ, NY, OH, PA, SC & TN. In addition, the company’s rental portfolio consists of approximately 11,000 units (an increase of 700 homes in the last 12 months).

Also, UMH is well positioned for growth with 3,500 existing vacant lots to fill and over 2,300 vacant acres on which to build approximately 9,300 future lots.

One key differentiator for UMH is its higher exposure to home rentals (not just land leases), which requires greater operational intensity and offers greater upside from occupancy gains and infill. As shown below, the average monthly rent is $1,044, which supports the affordability attributes referenced earlier.

Keep in mind that UMH tends to offer a higher yield but with slightly more operational complexity and risk than SUI and ELS.

Finally, there’s Flagship Communities, a Canadian REIT that is one of the Midwest region's largest developers of residential manufactured housing communities located throughout KY, OH, IN, West VA, TN, AK, IL, and MO.

As the smallest peer, Flagship owns 87 communities (16,920 lots) with an average lot rent of $483.00 (+7.7% over 2024). The company has maintained stable occupancy (around 83%) and best-in-class revenue and NOI growth (+10.9% and +11.0%, respectively). As you can see below, Flagship has generated best-in-class revenue and NOI growth:

As you will see below, Flagship scores relatively well in our “wide moat” growth scoring model.

So, without further ado, let’s dig deeper into our moat model…

Moats Matter

In case you missed it, I previously provided “moat” models for the net lease and industrial sectors, and I will continue this sector-by-sector journey in hopes of building out a systematic “wide moat” model for all REITs.

Weighted Average Cost of Capital

In previous articles, I highlighted the superior cost of capital for Agree Realty (ADC), Essential Properties (EPRT), and Terrano Realty (TRNO). As shown below, Equity Lifestyle (ELS) has the lowest WACC in the manufactured housing sector.

The Balance Sheet

Within the net lease and industrial sectors, Realty Income (O), Agree Realty, and Prologis (PLD) have the best balance sheets, and within the manufactured housing sector, Sun Communities has the top spot (with a moat score of 5). Equity Lifestyle also enjoys a strong balance sheet, while Flagship has the lowest moat score (of 2).

Growth

As I pointed out in the last (industrial REIT) article, “I decided to include both historical growth and consensus growth in this Industrial REIT article, which (in my opinion) provides an improved 'moat' score.” As mentioned previously, Flagship has superior growth, which is the reason the company has the highest “most” score of 5. The other three MH REITs have growth scores of 6.1%, which.

Performance

UMH gets the top spot for performance, primarily due to a 91% post-COVID bump; however, the company has been the worst performer YTD. Notably, Flagship jumped by 32% in 2025 and has since underperformed YTD.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, I find Equity Lifestyle the most attractive given its discount relative to normal valuation. Sun Communities is also attractive; however, shares are almost fairly valued. Given my knowledge of Flagship, I like the company a lot, and it deserves more attention (and perhaps capital) given its current value (14.3x) and modest payout ratio.

How to Play It?

Now, as you can see below, Equity Lifestyle has the best moat score – based on fundamentals and value. If you want the safest bet, I would go with ELS. However, Flagship has piqued my interest, and although it’s a Canadian ticker, its returns can be affected by exchange rate fluctuations.

If the CAD weakens vs. the USD, your total return may suffer, but the growth is compelling, and the company pays a monthly dividend.

At its core, manufactured housing is exactly the kind of business Warren Buffett has always gravitated toward: simple, durable, and built on real-world necessity.

Manufactured housing isn’t flashy, but it sits at the intersection of affordability, scarcity, and demographic tailwinds, three forces that are unlikely to reverse anytime soon.

Whether you lean toward the stability of ELS, the scale of SUI, the yield and upside of UMH, or the growth profile of Flagship, the underlying thesis remains the same: this is a sector where demand is structural and supply is constrained.

As always, thank you for reading and commenting, and I look forward to your comments below.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Slightly Warmer Beer To The King
data.description:
Investment Thesis

All eyes are on King Charles' state visit to the United States, his first as a sovereign. News headlines have highlighted the contrast between the amicable reception at the White House and the rising tensions between President Trump and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer. Back home, Ed Miliband, the Secretary of Energy and Net Zero in His Majesty's Government, spurred fury and ridicule when the ministry rolled out a tool that "optimizes" fridge temperature levels to help pubs (bars) and other hospitality businesses reduce electricity consumption amid rising energy prices. This came after the country became the latest to declare a state of energy emergency in the aftermath of the Strait of Hormuz disruptions.

Investors are grappling with more serious issues than a slightly warmer beer in the UK. The fluidity of the Iran situation has created noise that I believe has distracted investors from more important secular trends. For example, the flight to safety has suppressed global equities and boosted the US dollar, breaking two secular trends that remain very much in play. The high equity valuations in the US and the ballooning public debt have created a secular rotation trend away from US equity into global stocks.

This trend will only accelerate. Emerging economies are set to benefit from the democratization of know-how through the advent of Large Language Models "LLMs", which I expect will create seismic cultural and economic changes in developing countries that far exceed those happening in developed countries, including the US, where the tech was invented in the first place. The irony is that Sam Altman's deviation from OpenAI's philanthropic origins is being examined in court, while the company's tech could become the single most powerful tool for narrowing the global wealth divide—a root cause of social injustice and various types of abuses.

Investors can capture these trends through the many global equity ETFs on the market. My preference is for ETFs tracking the FTSE Emerging Markets All Cap China Inclusion Index, such as the Vanguard Emerging Markets Stock Index Fund ETF (VWO). Here's why. First, the ETF is unhedged, offering exposure to a potentially weaker dollar by holding global equities in local currencies, possibly set to appreciate against the dollar as investors flee the US public debt mess and high equity valuations. Secondly, it includes a diverse set of developing market equities, capturing know-how democratization trends and their positive impact on emerging economies. Thirdly, it is cap-weighted, meaning that successful, large companies are rewarded with bigger representation in the index. Fourth, the fund has a low expense ratio of just 0.07%.

China Exposure Risk

China's political risk premium seems to be decreasing, providing an accommodative tailwind to the VWO ETF.

China and Taiwan represent roughly 56% of VWO's weight. This creates risk, given the potential hostilities between the two. China considers Taiwan part of its territory. However, this situation also creates opportunities. The general consensus in Europe is that while the US remains its strongest ally, the relationship needs insurance coverage in case it breaks, and China seems to be selling the policy. Earlier this month, the Chinese Commerce Minister said that China and the EU's dispute over EVs has reached a soft landing. Multiple European leaders have visited China in recent months, a manifestation of the closer relationship between the EU and China and alignment over key issues, such as support for the United Nations and international law.

Moody's rating raised the outlook for China, citing a resilient economy in the face of tariffs.

Beyond China and Taiwan, VWO includes multiple smaller economies that are poised for systematic cultural and economic change because of the advent of AI. These include economies that lack key infrastructure systems, with AI poised to fill in these structural gaps. These transformative benefits could spur growth much faster than the value added that the same technology brings to other established economies.

Last year, the South African equity market, which represents between 4% to 5% of VWO, was the second fastest growing market in the world. Middle Eastern markets represent roughly 8% of the ETF, including Saudi Arabia (3.6%) and the United Arab Emirates (1.8%). These countries have been embarking on ambitious and transformative socioeconomic initiatives, particularly in Saudi Arabia, starting from the lifting of the women's driving ban in 2018, and the more recent Saudi Vision 2030.

India grew by 8.2% in the first half of FY 2025/2026, one of the fastest in the G20 group.

The dollar is in a Long-Term Decline

President Trump's push for lower interest rates is often pitched as a driver of economic growth. I don't think that's the main motive. Government finances are being stressed by the high interest rates. Servicing public debt is now the second-largest account in the government's budget after social services, as government borrowing reached $39 trillion last month.

There is no political consensus over how to solve the problem, and the world is gradually realizing that this could be a systemic issue. This fiscal issue is putting pressure on the US dollar, whose global dominance rests on one single factor: confidence. Without confidence in the US economy, the US dollar as a reserve and international trade currency starts to make less sense.

Note: Since the start of 2025, the US dollar index has lost 9.2% of its value.

US High Valuation

In my view, investors should not wait for the perfect geopolitical conditions to start placing bets on the return of the established secular trends that have been disrupted by the war. That seems to be the crowd wisdom in the US, with the NASDAQ-100 (NDX), S&P 500 (SP500), and Dow Jones (DJI) at all-time highs, as investors bet on the continuation of an AI-fueled growth.

Emerging markets haven't yet matched the US markets, a manifestation of the heightened, but more importantly, temporary geopolitical risks that tend to impact emerging economies more. For those wishing to start betting on the end of the Iran hostilities and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, a better alternative would be emerging markets.

The VWO holdings now have a weighted average P/E ratio of 15.8x, much lower than the NDX (33.4), SP500 (25.4x), and the DJI (23.6x).

Final Thoughts and Risks

Most countries impacted or involved in the Iran conflict have strong incentives to end the hostilities fast for good reasons, including the midterm elections in the US, an exhausted Iranian military, and thirsty pub-goers asking for a cold beer. It is not too early to start thinking of the day after.

At the start of this year, VWO was outperforming the three major US market indexes, and up until the war, the gap was widening. Why? 1) Global equities were rising, benefiting from a rotational trade that saw investors fleeing expensive US equities and technology concentration into less expensive, less concentrated global equities. 2) The US dollar was weakening, as investors realized that the US debt issue might be more structural than once thought.

Emerging markets might offer a favorable risk-adjusted return as Trump continues his efforts to reshape the US's position in the world.

data.title: Best-Of-Breed Infrastructure Dividend Growth Machines Yielding Up To 7.6%
data.description:
Infrastructure (UTF) is one of my favorite sectors to look for dividend growth opportunities because it combines all of the major ingredients that I look for when investing. First, many infrastructure businesses have durable and defensive business models due to serving vital functions for the economy and being highly contracted and/or regulated. For example, utilities (XLU) companies like NextEra Energy (NEE) are highly regulated and/or contracted with their assets for lengthy terms, often with investment-grade counterparties, and therefore able to generate resilient cash flows regardless of the economic environment.

Many infrastructure companies are also able to command strong investment-grade credit ratings, given how durable and defensive their cash flow profile is, which enables them to carry a reasonable amount of leverage while still having a strong investment-grade credit rating. Since their underlying cash flows are often bond-like in nature, they have durable defensive business models and strong balance sheets. This then enables them to be attractive dividend payers and consistent dividend growers, because the strong cash flow and financial profiles enable them to consistently return a set amount to shareholders.

As long as they are prudently managed, they are often able to increase those payouts over time at a rate that meets or beats inflation, since many of them have inflation protections and/or inflation escalators on their cash flows and can invest in attractive growth opportunities to grow cash flows at least at a rate that keeps up with inflation and population growth. We can see this in many infrastructure companies, such as Enbridge (ENB), which has grown its dividend consistently year after year for over a quarter century, as well as names like Clearway Energy (CWEN, CWEN.A) Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP, BEPC) and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP, BIPC), all of which have impressive dividend growth track records.

It is simply a matter of waiting until one of these well-run, durable, and defensive businesses with strong balance sheets and impressive dividend growth goes on sale to the point where they offer an attractive combination of yield and growth. With this in view, today I'm going to discuss two dividend growth machines that are actually at the fund level, rather than individual stocks in the infrastructure space, that I think are some of my favorites to invest in for an attractive combination of yield, growth, and ultimately total returns over time.

The Midstream Transformation Story

The first opportunity I'm going to discuss is the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP). At first glance, AMLP's long-term chart looks unremarkable, if not bad, as the ETF's price has declined by almost one-third since its inception in 2010.

However, there are some important nuances to keep in mind, as the story has been much different over the past five years for the fund, as it has shot up 58.33% over that time. Namely, the entire state of the midstream MLP sector has changed dramatically over time.

Early on, MLPs had a much more aggressive and unsustainable model, where they would issue debt consistently to fuel aggressive growth spending and then would return virtually all their cash flow to investors. However, when energy prices crashed, concerns about overbuilding began to abound, not to mention the ESG investing movement emerged; all of these things weighed heavily on MLPs and prevented them from being able to raise sufficient capital, thereby leading them to slash their payouts, including some like Kinder Morgan (KMI), Energy Transfer (ET), and Plains All American (PAA, PAGP), in particular, in the wake of the COVID-19 outbreak, which caused energy prices to plummet.

However, since then, many of these companies have become much more conservative in how they structure their balance sheets, including paying down debt and deleveraging aggressively, emphasizing free cash flow generation, securing strong investment-grade credit ratings, and now putting not only their distributions but also their distribution growth profiles on a much firmer footing. This has been evidenced by the sharp rebound in the sector over the past five years, which has led to AMLP significantly outperforming the broader market over that period, despite it also being a very strong performance period for the S&P 500 as well.

AMLP's distribution growth has also been very strong recently, with it growing its payout for the past four years in a row, and for the past three years, its dividend CAGR has been a whopping 9.91%. It remains very attractive today, with its top holdings including the likes of PAA and ET, which, despite their checkered history, are some of the more attractively priced names that combine high current yields with solid distribution growth profiles and strong balance sheets, as well as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), which is the gold standard for the sector. EPD has the highest credit rating at A- in the entire sector and an attractive combination of yield, growth, and unit repurchases.

Meanwhile, AMLP yields 7.54% on a trailing 12-month basis today, which, when combined with its strong dividend growth momentum, makes it a very powerful infrastructure investment that focuses on energy pipelines (MLPX) in particular. Additionally, I expect U.S.-based midstream, which is what AMLP focuses on, to benefit from increased energy export demand from countries in Europe and Asia in the wake of the Iran conflict and the Strait of Hormuz, as I recently detailed here. Therefore, I think AMLP combines an attractive combination of high yield, strong recent price performance, and dividend growth momentum, as well as potential macro tailwinds from increased export demand and AI data center demand for natural gas pipelines.

A One-Stop Infrastructure Income Shop

The other infrastructure name that I've been highly impressed with over time is the Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG). Note that it is a CEF rather than an ETF, and therefore its price-to-NAV ratio can fluctuate over time. However, its price-to-NAV ratio has remained fairly stable over time, making it ETF-like in nature, since its 52-week low is just a 4.48% discount to its NAV. Its 52-week high is just a 1.71% premium to NAV. Today it trades at a 0.28% discount to NAV, which means that it is trading right around its NAV and is not particularly a good buy or expensive either.

Its current distribution yield is 5.66%, with distributions paid monthly. One of the things I like about the fund is that, for an actively managed CEF, its expense ratio before leverage expense of 0.88% is actually quite reasonable. In fact, it is roughly in line with AMLP's 0.85% expense ratio, despite AMLP being passively managed, so you're getting the active management here essentially for free relative to another infrastructure fund like AMLP, though it is higher than some other passively managed infrastructure funds out there. Regardless, when you combine that with a fairly modest leverage ratio of 18.5%, that is enough to juice the yield on the total returns of the fund without putting it in too much drawdown and margin call risk in the event of a market crash. I think the fund is well structured.

This is further evidenced by its impressive long-term distribution track record of sustaining or growing its distribution ever since the fund's inception back in 2004, while it also recently hiked its distribution over the past year by over 5%, from 19 cents per month to 20 cents per month.

The fund's long-term total return performance has also validated its approach, with it outperforming utility stocks over the long term.

Power, Pipelines, And The AI Boom

One of the biggest draws of UTG, in addition to its attractive monthly payouts, strong long-term dividend growth track record, and attractive long-term total return outperformance, is the fact that it gives you a one-stop shop to an actively managed and well-diversified portfolio of infrastructure. It has exposure to names spanning the utilities, general energy infrastructure, and power generation assets.

For example, one of its largest positions is Vistra Corporation (VST) and Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG), both of which are power producers that are expected to be big beneficiaries from AI-related energy demand. Meanwhile, they also hold a large position in CenterPoint Energy (CNP), which is a regulated utility that should benefit significantly from growing power demand. At the same time, they also have exposure to Deutsche Telekom (DTEGY) and other unique infrastructure assets that should provide valuable diversification for investors looking for well-rounded infrastructure exposure.

The Bottom Line For Yield-Hungry Investors

While neither of these funds is cheap when it comes to expense ratios, both are somewhat sensitive to energy price and interest rate volatility, and UTG in particular does not look undervalued at the moment. Both of these funds still offer a very attractive combination of current yield and dividend growth while also enjoying favorable macro tailwinds from things like:

  • The risk of elevated inflation.

  • Growing energy demand across the globe for U.S.-sourced energy, accelerated by the Strait of Hormuz crisis.

  • The AI-related power demand boom.

Thus, for investors who are looking for well-diversified and proven sources of attractive current yield and dividend growth over time and don't want to dig into the weeds of individual companies, I think that AMLP and UTG are very attractive options to consider. As a result, they are among some of our favorite alternatives to suggest to investors who want to avoid individual K-1-issuing stocks in our 7–8% yielding, total return outperforming, below-market-beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Dovish-Leaning Hold By The Bank of Canada
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US and Francesco Pesole, FX Strategist

Jobs and USMCA concerns keep BoC slightly dovish

The Bank of Canada left its policy rate at 2.25% as expected, but the overall tone of the accompanying statement leant somewhat dovish relative to market expectations. The BoC acknowledged the impact higher energy prices were having on headline inflation and inflation expectations, and Governor Tiff Macklem said that a hike might be needed if oil prices were to remain elevated for a long period. However, the Bank also emphasised that core inflation has behaved well and that the proportion of components of CPI that are above target has declined.

Meanwhile, the jobs market is still described as “soft” with weak hiring and job losses seen in sectors exposed to US tariffs. Growth projections also remain modest, with 1.2% GDP growth predicted for this year, 1.6% next, and 1.7% in 2028. Those 2027 and 2028 forecasts are a tenth of a percentage point below the consensus growth estimates.

While higher energy prices are a boon for Canada’s economy, given it is a major net producer of oil and gas, the ongoing uncertainty about trade policy continues to hold back sentiment. The coming evaluations of the USMCA trade deal between Canada, the US, and Mexico and the potential for the US to put more of a squeeze on trade partners prompted Governor Macklem to warn that if there are significant rule changes that harm Canada, then a rate cut may be required.

Our call is still for no hikes

As such, the BoC seems to be hedging its bets a little. For now, the Bank is prepared to “look through the war’s immediate impact on inflation”. Assuming oil prices come down, and US tariffs remain unchanged, “a policy rate close to current settings looks appropriate”. The upside risk stems from a prolonged period of elevated inflation costs that starts to feed through into broader price increases, while the downside risk is increased trade restrictions with the US.

In terms of our view, our base case is that the Middle East situation will gradually ease, we see a slow resumption of tanker flows through the Strait of Hormuz over the next couple of months, and energy prices do indeed start to come down. We also see the potential for a tough period of talks over the USMCA deal that will leave a cloud of uncertainty over the economy and weigh on the jobs market. In this situation, we continue to favour stable policy rates through to the end of the year.

Dovish BoC restrains CAD

USD/CAD briefly traded on the strong side after today’s BoC announcement, although market pricing didn’t shift meaningfully. Expectations remain for a 25bp rate hike at the October meeting, so more hawkish than our baseline call.

Today’s relatively dovish BoC endorses our end 2Q call for 1.37 in USD/CAD. We do see some downside risks to that forecast given resilience in equity markets and oil prices remaining more elevated than longer. But for now, we are assuming there will be a period of elevated uncertainty around USMCA renegotiations in June, leading to upward volatility in the pair.

We expect better CAD performance in 2H26, when a new USMCA deal could ultimately be agreed upon and the Federal Reserve should cut rates. We expect 1.34 by year-end in USD/CAD, with risks to the downside.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
